does: This paper introduces MRJ-Agent, an automated jailbreak agent designed to expose vulnerabilities in large language models through multi-round dialogue interactions. Unlike prior work focused on single-turn attacks or manual prompt templates, the agent uses a principled approach to construct sequences of queries that progressively manipulate the model into producing harmful or unethical outputs across multiple conversation turns. The method emphasizes stealthiness—crafting attacks that appear natural and contextually coherent while gradually eroding the model's safety guardrails. The paper demonstrates that multi-round jailbreaks can be significantly more effective than single-round attempts, revealing that LLMs' safety mechanisms are vulnerable to gradual manipulation over dialogue sequences.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research examining LLM vulnerabilities to adversarial prompts and jailbreak techniques. It extends prior single-turn jailbreaking methods by recognizing that human-AI interaction is inherently multi-turn, making dialogue-based attacks more realistic and practical. The paper responds to limitations in existing approaches that rely on manually engineered templates or generic prompt engineering, which struggle with the compositional complexity of multi-round conversations. It connects to research on adversarial robustness in NLP, iterative attack strategies, and the broader safety evaluation of large models, positioning multi-round dialogue as an underexplored but critical attack surface that prior threat models have largely overlooked.
enables: This research enables more comprehensive safety auditing and red-teaming of large language models by identifying vulnerabilities that manifest only through extended interactions. The agent-based approach provides a foundation for automated, scalable jailbreak testing that could inform the design of more robust safety mechanisms and alignment techniques. Future work can build on this framework to develop defenses against multi-round manipulation, better contextual safety mechanisms that maintain consistency across dialogue turns, and improved evaluation protocols for LLM deployment. The work also highlights the need for safety training approaches that account for adversarial manipulation across multiple turns, potentially leading to dialogue-aware alignment methods and more sophisticated red-teaming infrastructure for responsible AI deployment.
